t: Intellectual assessment (WISC-V, Stanford-Binet 5, non-verbal options — Leiter-3, Mullen).
Medical and genetic assessment — chromosomal microarray (CMA), Fragile X (FMR1), selective trio exome; EEG when seizure suspicion exists; MRI in clinical suspicion (microcephaly, regression).
Comorbidity screening: ADHD, anxiety, depression, OCD, sleep, eating, GI.
General cognitive deficit; social reciprocity corresponds to intellectual level. Can be comorbid.
Deficit in the use of social language; no restricted-repetitive patterns of behavior.
Arises from early psychosocial deprivation; potential for recovery in appropriate environment.
Usually manifests in adulthood; psychotic-like thinking.
Ego-dystonic obsessions and compulsions; rituals in ASP are ego-syntonic.
Social avoidance anxiety-based; social interest preserved.
Not speaking in specific contexts; normal speech in home context.
M-CHAT-R/F (16–30 months), SCQ (4+ years, parent questionnaire), SRS-2 (Social Responsiveness Scale).
Mullen Scales of Early Learning, Bayley-III/IV (young age), WISC-V, Leiter-3 (non-verbal).
Conners for ADHD, SCARED for anxiety, PHQ-9 adaptations for depression.
Chromosomal microarray analysis (CMA—diagnostic yield ~10–20%); Fragile X (FMR1).

trio exome sequencing (WES — diagnostic yield of 25–40% if CMA is negative); MECP2 (for suspected Rett syndrome in females); metabolic (lactate, ammonia, amino acids — if regression is present); lead level; TSH.
EEG — in case of seizure, regression, bizarre behavior episodes.
Brain MRI — focal neurological signs, micro/macrocephaly, regression.
Hearing test — mandatory in all cases (audiometry, ABR).
8.1 General principles (NICE CG170 · AAP 2020 · AACAP 2014 consensus)
ASD cannot be 'cured' — individual development, adaptation, and quality of life are improved.
Claims of "cure" and "full recovery" are not scientifically based; the goal is functional skills, communication, independence, quality of life.
First-line—effect greatest between ages 18 months to 6 years (Reichow B. et al. Cochrane 2018, EIBI/ESDM meta-analysis).
— based on the principles of behavior-development in play and daily contexts; more flexible than the classical discrete-transition version of ADHD. Included in this group:
Joint Attention, Symbolic Play, Engagement, and Regulation (JASPER)
Detailed description of methods — in the 'Treatment methodologies' section.
— Hanen "More Than Words", PACT (Pickles A., Green J. et al. Lancet 2010, 2016 follow-up — 6-year effect sustained), PRT parent version. Most cost-effective approach in resource-limited settings.
— for older children and adolescents; PEERS program (UCLA, Laugeson E.A.) — also effective in adults.
— TEACCH (Treatment and Education of Autistic and related Communication-handicapped Children, Schopler E. North Carolina); IEP / EHCP individual education plan.
In minimally verbal patients — Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) — PECS (Picture Exchange Communication System), speech-generating devices, tablet-based applications.

ADHD (stimulants, Cortese 2018), anxiety (CBT modification — Storch E.A. RCTs), depression (CBT, SSRIs), epilepsy (standard antiepileptic), sleep (sleep hygiene, melatonin — RCT evidence Goldman S.E. J Clin Sleep Med 2014).

There is no FDA-approved medication for the core symptoms of ASD. For behavioral aggression and irritability — risperidone (5–17 years, FDA approval 2006) and aripiprazole (6–17 years, FDA approval 2009). Both are evidence-based (McCracken J.T. et al. NEJM 2002 RCT — risperidone), but the side effect burden is significant (metabolic, EPS). Behavioral intervention is first-line.

diagnosis by a multidisciplinary group; social communication interventions are first-line; parent programs are superior; antipsychotics only when behavioral interventions fail; caution against claims of actual "treatment".
18 and 24 months universal screening; early intervention (<3 years); integrated medical and behavioral management.
emphasis on diagnosis-specific educational placement, comorbidity treatment, family support.
resource-limited parent training program for low and middle income countries; widely adapted across countries (Salomone E. et al. Autism 2019).

Learning method based on behavioral principles; shaping social, communicative, and adaptive skills, reducing challenging behavior. Applied in discrete trial training (DTT) or naturalistic format. Part of adult autistic community criticizes classical form of ABA (autonomy, distress).

— Structured intensive intervention based on ASD at early age (25–40 hours per week); target — social, communicative, and adaptive skills. Evidence: Reichow B., Hume K., Barton E.E., Boyd B.A. Cochrane 2018 — small-to-medium effect on IQ, language, and adaptive behavior; current claims should be approached with caution.
Early Start Denver Model (ESDM) — Rogers S., Dawson G.

Naturalistic developmental behavioral intervention for 12–48 months; learning within the context of play and daily activities. Evidence: Dawson G. et al. Pediatrics 2010 RCT n=48 — significant improvement in IQ, language, and adaptive behavior, diagnosis change at age 6 (Dawson G. et al. JAACAP 2012 follow-up). Parent version (P-ESDM) Rogers S.J. et al. JAACAP 2012.
Joint Attention, Symbolic Play, Engagement, and Regulation (JASPER) — Kasari (Kasari C.)

— Intervention targeting joint attention and symbolic play development; 12–60 months. Evidence: Kasari C. et al. JAMA Pediatr 2014, J Consult Clin Psychol 2010 — significant improvement in joint attention and expressive language.
